title: 3D Materiality: Textiles  7-week class runs 1/11-2/26
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: 3D Materiality: Textiles  7-week class runs 3/1-4/23
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Information Design
description: The goal of this studio course is to introduce students to basic principles of information design. Students will spend the semester exploring methods of collecting and categorizing data in order to reveal connections and create a better understanding of complex subjects. Assignments and presentations will explore traditional forms of data visualization – maps, diagrams, charts, timelines, interfaces and graphs – in both historical and contemporary contexts. Assignments will require both work in the field and within a studio environment. Graphic Design Major requirement. 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Interaction Design
description: Interaction Design is not new: books, print, and wayfinding sign systems are examples of interfaces that have been in place for centuries. Now, websites, tablets, phones, and mobile apps dominate the landscape, and the future might hold interfaces that are invisible or automatic or interactive only through voice. Our common definition of interface refers to the point at which two systems, subjects or organizations meet and interact. In design we focus on creating that point of interaction or point of delivery between a person and the interface. However, as technology shifts our attention from designing one-of-a-kind artifacts to the design of systems and experiences, this studio course will ask you to expand your notion of and approach to designing interfaces.  This is a visual design course to emphasize cross-platform design experiences; Issues of hierarchy, color, size, readability, graphical user interface, architecture and the pragmatics of interface communication is explored while utilizing current technologies, such as HTML, CSS and JavaScript. Projects will look at the web as a dynamic visual environment. Graphic Design Major requirement. 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week. Major requirement.

title: Introduction to Drawing
description: This course introduces drawing from perception, including techniques for measurement, contour, massing, volume, and rendering illumination through value. Compositional skills such as placement and figure/ground interaction are stressed.  Drawing materials include vine charcoal, compressed charcoal, stamp, and india ink; subject matter includes still life, interior, landscape, and the figure.

title: Anatomical Drawing
description: This class is an in-depth study of the internal structure of the human figure as it affects the external form, allowing students to develop a working vocabulary of surface muscle groups and skeletal landmarks as they apply to drawing the figure. Students complete weekly research projects designed to compliment classroom topics.   Class time is used primarily for study from the live model and critique.  Application of knowledge gained throughout the course to individual aesthetic and conceptual concerns is encouraged.

title: 4D: Space & Temporality
description: This course investigates modes of practice beyond the creation of objects. Space and Temporality focuses on verbs; process as opposed to product. Space & Temporality cultivates an understanding of design and composition within time and space. The class explores these principles through developing an understanding of the body as a dynamic object in time and space, lens-based seeing, light, – 9 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] audio, sequential and process-based drawing, and the moving image. Processes and materials include both conventional and digital approaches. The course blends multiple working methodologies, strategies and tools to emphasize the specific learning objectives. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Photography Majors Studio
description: This critique class, supplemented with appropriate readings and slide presentations, is designed to allow each junior to explore the forms and content of their own work. Major standing: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Photography Majors Studio
description: This advanced project allows each student to work independently, with bi-weekly group and individual critiques, to produce a body of work that can then be refined into a final presentation form--portfolio, exhibition, book, installation, etc.--for the Senior Thesis Exhibition. Editing, sequencing, and presentation methods are covered in detail. Major standing: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

Space & Temporality cultivates an understanding of design and composition within time and space. The class explores these principles through developing an understanding of the body as a dynamic object in time and space, lens-based seeing, light, – 9 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] audio, sequential and process-based drawing, and the moving image. Processes and materials include both conventional and digital approaches. The course blends multiple working methodologies, strategies and tools to emphasize the specific learning objectives. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Introduction to the Discipline
description: This course looks at a range of painting strategies and investigates painting through its histories, as a technical skill, and as an intellectual and philosophical project.Intro to the Discipline is complementary to Junior Studio.  Readings, presentations, videos and discussions will establish a foundation of critical ideas, vocabulary, models, and approaches pertinent to the serious study of painting. Topics include the development of content by looking at genre, process, materials and formats; a broad range of histories; painting in the expanded field; and the curation of exhibitions. This Studio Seminar course provides the ground for you to more clearly define your own practice and relationship to the various histories and contexts through which your work gains meaning, particularly those contexts that have defined contemporary painting. This is a mandatory course for all Painting Majors. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 3 hours/week.

title: Sculpture: Junior Major Studio
description: This course emphasizes the making of sculpture: inspiration, identifying material resources, developing technical skills, craftsmanship and critical awareness of visual and structural integrity. Students will complete three works and supporting materials in the form of three-dimensional studies, experiments and drawings are expected. Students choose materials and subjective references, and works may incorporate resources and inspiration from many other disciplines. Students develop productive work habits and learn to take charge of their direction and keep to a schedule. Technical assistance is available to help with new processes. Students work in all three studios of the department. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Introduction to Drawing
description: This course introduces drawing from perception, including techniques for measurement, contour, massing, volume, and rendering illumination through value. Compositional skills such as placement and figure/ground interaction are stressed.  Drawing materials include vine charcoal, compressed charcoal, stamp, and india ink; subject matter includes still life, interior, landscape, and the figure.

title: Honors: Introduction to Drawing
description: The Honors section of Introduction to Drawing is a rigorous and demanding course designed for the advanced student. This course introduces drawing from perception, including techniques for measurement, contour, massing, volume, and rendering illumination through value. Compositional skills such as placement and figure/ground interaction are stressed. Drawing materials include vine charcoal, compressed charcoal, stamp, and India ink; subject matter includes still life, interior, landscape, and the figure.

title: Water Media
description: This course examines the relationship of specific water-based painting media and techniques to the content and activity of painting. The sequencing and techniques of water-based media are often opposite and / or drastically different than those of oil painting. Significant developments in the acrylic paint industry facilitate completely different potential for creative painterly expression (new mediums, grounds, techniques, and paints). Students will explore and combine a variety of water media with the expectation of discovering a new, personal approach to creating images and developing visual ideas.  Students are initially taught the specific properties and use of watercolor, gouache, and acrylic and then work to combine these media with pastel, watercolor pencil, watercolor crayons, and tempera. Demonstrations, lectures, and art historical references will impart a basic understanding of methods and materials.  Emphasis will be placed on facility with material, understanding of formal issues, color use, and quality and range of investigations.

title: Painting Majors Studio
description: Students develop productive studio habits, explore personal themes and begin to define their own visual language. They are encouraged to investigate a variety of materials and processes. Trips to artist studios, major museums and galleries supplement the educational experience. Critiques, which include discussions, lectures and demonstrations, and a variety of visiting artists are held regularly. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Perpetual Play
description: This course is an introduction to the tools, skills, and possibilities of graphic design. Emphasis is placed on expression and communication, as students use illustration, typography, and play itself to shape a lively graphic language. With both analog and digital tools, students will explore various materials with an emphasis on ideation and working methods that combine the practical with the expressive. Projects range from quick class workshops to in-depth explorations.

title: History as Narrative Storytelling
description: The historian C.L.R. James famously said "There is no drama like the drama of history." The writing of history is the writing of narratives - winners and losers, high stakes battles and romances, and world changing events. In this course, we will study history as narrative and the process of producing a strong and compelling story and narrative. We will read historical narratives from around the world and examine how narratives about a historical event shift and change as new storytellers take on events. We will also take a deep dive into how to craft narratives - both as historians and artists. In doing so, we will explore how the power of narrative storytelling can help change the world.

title: Thinking with Drawing
description: This course focuses on using drawing to get to a core idea. Drawing can be used as a process of reflection, discovery, proposal, and presentation; a tool for thinking. While drawings themselves may not be the endgame of artists’ and designers’ research and creativity, the practice can be the starting point and conversation support for an idea and an integral part of the ideation process. Think storyboard, object or project proposal, working drawing, layout, thumbnail, and whatever else bridges the gap between the artist’s imagination and the need for the maker to work through a concept. A drawing can be used to propose things that aren't quite visible or don’t yet exist. Through the invitation of visiting faculty, students are exposed to a wide range of studio practices that use drawing (e.g., furniture making, metal fabrication, ceramics, illustration and design concepts, set design, architecture, digital media, film, etc.). Projects include prompted exercises that lead up to summative personal independent work.

title: Methods and Media in Illustration
description: Students learn and build technical skills using a variety of water based materials such as acrylic, gouache, and watercolor as they apply to illustration. Students will gain an understanding of the effective use of color through painting and color mixing exercises. Focus on representational and observation.

title: Introduction to Comics
description: This course is offered for students interested in using comics and sequential art as a means of communication and personal expression. Comics is a medium that is uniquely personal and combines the disciplines of writing and illustration to convey a narrative. Students will apply drawing and graphic design skills to develop signature styles in short and longer-term projects, while exploring possibilities of the medium by experimenting in storytelling, page layout, drawing styles and different media. There is an emphasis on self-publishing.

title: Introduction to Drawing
description: This course introduces drawing from perception, including techniques for measurement, contour, massing, volume, and rendering illumination through value. Compositional skills such as placement and figure/ground interaction are stressed.  Drawing materials include vine charcoal, compressed charcoal, stamp, and india ink; subject matter includes still life, interior, landscape, and the figure.

title: Slip Casting and Press Molding
description: This course emphasizes developing skills and technical knowledge in ceramic mold-making processes. Clay is a very flexible material; it can be shaped and formed by numerous methods and techniques. In this course, students will explore the unique properties of liquid casting slip and plaster mold-making. Topics include plaster molds for ceramic slip casting, block molds, two-part, and complex molds. Lecture and research topics encompass mold-making and casting in art and industry, historical applications, and contemporary materials/processes. Students will learn to create clay prototypes for simple one-piece molds and learn about more complex multi- piece plaster molds taken from other prototypes. The concept of “multiplicity” will be explored by using the molds to produce multiple objects and then proceed to creating complex assembled forms composed of many slip-cast parts. This course will introduce a range of functional and sculptural forms.

title: Ceramics IV: Major Studio
description: Students develop a mature body of work preparing for the Senior Thesis Exhibition. In addition, students are offered the necessary instruction and support needed to pursue in-depth explorations of ideas and processes of their choosing. Demonstrations, readings, discussions, and critical reviews continue studio work's conceptual, aesthetic, and technical development. Upon successfully completing the senior studio, students are ready to begin their professional careers. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Ceramics III: Major Studio
description: This course is the primary studio component for the spring semester of the junior year in the ceramics major. The course builds upon the experiences of CE 301, and it continues to foster the development of a personal sense of direction. Through a structure of self- directed assignments, Juniors learn to take responsibility for self-directed work individually. During the semester, students increase their vocabulary of skills and concepts. Students also continue to relate their work to historical and contemporary issues. Additionally, students gain more experience in operating various kilns and explore various clay bodies appropriate for their work. A combination of group discussions, demonstrations, shared and individual crits, and one-on-one dialogue further augment the growth and maturation of personally driven studio work. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Senior Synthesis
description: This course is taken in the final semester of the senior year. It is an integral course with the studio practice. Students will be led through a guided research and writing process to identify and explore what their inspirations are for their studio work, how those interests are played out historically and what their relevance is to contemporary art and culture. The class will culminate in a fully articulated written thesis that explains and details their thesis work. Students will present their ideas at various points throughout the semester and conversations will be held about the ideas underlying their work both in the classroom and in individualized studio visits. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 3 hours/week.

title: 3D Materiality: Ceramics. week 1-7
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. title: English Composition
description: Reading and writing are essential parts of college learning and successful art & design practice. In this class, students will explore a variety of approaches to reading and writing, including close reading and textual analysis, critical and comparative analysis, and reflective, argumentative writing. Students will be introduced to three major genres of text: the novel; the graphic novel; and poetry.

title: English Composition
description: Reading and writing are essential parts of college learning and successful art & design practice. In this class, students will explore a variety of approaches to reading and writing, including close reading and textual analysis, critical and comparative analysis, and reflective, argumentative writing. Students will be introduced to three major genres of text: the novel; the graphic novel; and poetry.

title: (W) Writing Minor Capstone
description: Writing Capstone is the capstone course of the Writing Minor. Each student will focus on an individual writing project totaling at least 100 pages: a novella; memoir; a play or series of plays; compilation of poetry; short story collection; compilation of essays/blog entries; graphic novel, etc. This individual writing project will be “workshopped” in a writing group of peer editors--fellow Writing Workshop class members--led by the professor.  Multiple drafts of each project will be required in response to the group’s feedback. The culmination of the course will be the publication of each student’s work, either through self-publishing or traditional publishing.

title: Anti-hegemonic Thought: Literature and Philosophy
description: This course examines writings from across the globe. From Latin America and the Caribbean to the Middle East, Africa and Oceania, Europe and North America, we will explore the largely open concept of “Anti-hegemonic Thought” through both creative fiction and nonfiction (poetry, short stories, autobiographies, etc.) whilst also looking into theoretical texts. During the semester, students will consider thematic and transnational contexts to explore multiple intersections of capitalism, class, gender, race, sexuality, imperialism, war, indigeneity, and settler colonialism (among other theoretical frameworks). Course texts focus on creative and critical works that imagine or create alternatives to enduring forms of capital, colonial, imperial, and racial violence.

title: (W) Writing Past Catharsis: Micro-Memoir
description: One doesn't have to live a long life to write a memoir; a memoir is not an autobiography. It is a snapshot from your life, a polaroid from a moment where everything changed. In this class, you will learn how to take experiences from your life--from wild and poignant experiences to ordinary and typical--and learn how to create a short memoir and/or collection of essays that is a work of well-crafted art. Because word after word after word equals power.

title: (PE) (S) Living Sustainably
description: Are you concerned about what is happening to the health of our planet? Do you find yourself feeling helpless to do anything about it? Then your first step should be to take sustainable living, the class that examines how our daily lives affect the normal functioning of our biosphere. We explore water, solid waste, single-use plastic, and discuss what you can do to live more sustainably and make a real difference!  This class includes field trips, guest speakers, instructions for hands-on activities such as making homemade laundry detergent, and the chance to incorporate your knowledge and interests into a work of art.

title: Methods and Media in Illustration
description: Students learn and build technical skills using a variety of water based materials such as acrylic, gouache, and watercolor as they apply to illustration. Students will gain an understanding of the effective use of color through painting and color mixing exercises. Focus on representational and observation.

title: Illustration Majors Studio
description: This course introduces students to new visual problems, illustration formats, and image making solutions. Students will develop original concepts built on research and observation as well as conceptual ideation and invention. Those concepts and ideas will be resolved while refining manual drawing skills and exploring various techniques for creating finished illustrations. This course will be based around various relevant assignments that explore both the expressive and representational nature of illustration, accompanied by discussion of contemporary illustration practices. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Special Topics: Shaders & VFX
description: From a crackling campfire in the dark, to the sparkle that bursts when you collect a powerup, to goopy green acid that bubbles around a villain’s lair, visual effects (VFX) are a crucial vehicle for delivering information about mood, threats, goals, and rewards to a player.  This course covers not only the tools used to author particle effects and shaders, but also the high level philosophies that drive the design process of such assets.  Part animation, part tech art, part painting, but explosively enjoyable throughout.

title: Intro to Game Development
description: This course will introduce students to the foundational aspects of game development.  Starting with elemental lessons that teach students the basics of working in game engines and give insights about the game industry on a whole, we’ll move into design- or theory- based projects about game mechanics and art production. Game development is a complex, multidisciplinary practice, and this course will serve as the bedrock for the rest of your gamedev journey here at MECA&D.

title: Sophomore Production Lab
description: Students in Sophomore Production Lab will use the knowledge and skills they have picked up over their first three semesters to create a short original animated film or game project. They will work individually or collaboratively to execute their creative vision while learning to budget their time as they navigate their way through a full production pipeline. Major requirement: 3 cr./semester; 6 Hrs/week.

title: Intermediate 3D Modeling & Animation
description: A fast-paced survey course for students who love digital 3d, this class builds upon the foundation of Intro to 3d Modeling and Animation.  Through demos, walk-throughs, and projects, students will explore topics such as high poly modeling, digital sculpting, PBR material generation, advanced rigging, and character animation.

title: Animation & Game Art Concepts and History
description: This course offers a historical and critical overview of animation and game art. Case studies in animation and game art will be presented and discussed. Assigned readings, playings, and viewings will introduce and illustrate the formal, conceptual, and kinetic norms of animation and narrative across media platforms. Students will create written and/or visual responses to each of the major themes of the course. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: (M) Intro to Digital Audio Recording
description: Students will learn digital audio production and music production using contemporary recording techniques and Digital Audio Workstation applications in MECA&D’s recording studio. The simple physics of sound will be demystified. Basic recording skills, including the use of microphones, outboard and plug-in audio processing and mixing will be emphasized. Project-based, hands-on exercises throughout the course will reinforce the concepts covered.

title: (M) Music Minor Capstone
description: This course is a culminating experience for seniors in the minor.  Students will build upon the skills and concepts introduced in the Music Minor to realize a project of substantial effort within a semester. Examples include: scoring a film/animation, producing a live performance, recording a 6-Song EP of original work, creating an installation that incorporates sound, or writing a 10-song collection. A statement of purpose and a project plan is required. Students work with a faculty advisor under an independent study arrangement. The faculty advisor and student meet prior, during and at the completion of the capstone project. Through this project, students will demonstrate their skills and understanding of how music and sound both affects and supports their art or how they have further developed within the field of sound/music. The concept and scope of the project must be approved by the Coordinator of the Bob Crewe Program in Art and Music.

title: Instrumental Lab: Keyboard  1 cr. class runs 7-week (1/11-2/26)
description: This course is designed for beginning to intermediate keyboardists. The class is a 7-week group lesson for developing your piano skills and understanding standard music notation. Students will use 88-note, piano-style keyboards during class time and have access to – 12 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] our practice rooms with the same equipment.

title: Typography II
description: This course is an advanced investigation into conventional and unconventional forms of typography. A variety of projects provide exposure to complex issues of organization, hierarchy, and sequence in traditional and contemporary typographic communication. Special attention is given to refinement, detail, and finishing skills in all forms of typographic design while understanding the significance of context and audience, as well as the voice and narrative potential of type. Graphic Design Major requirement. Graphic Design Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Art History Survey II
description: The second semester of the art history survey course combines a chronological overview of art works from the seventeenth century through up to 1960 with a rigorous investigation of the contexts in which art has been made. The course covers major art historical developments in the western and non-western worlds with an evaluation of their form, content, style, cultural meaning, and historical import. The course also introduces students to art historical vocabulary and various methods of art historical research. Each section meets twice a week for lecture and student discussion.

title: Art History Survey II
description: The second semester of the art history survey course combines a chronological overview of art works from the seventeenth century through up to 1960 with a rigorous investigation of the contexts in which art has been made. The course covers major art historical developments in the western and non-western worlds with an evaluation of their form, content, style, cultural meaning, and historical import. The course also introduces students to art historical vocabulary and various methods of art historical research. Each section meets twice a week for lecture and student discussion.

title: Sophomore Production Lab
description: Students in Sophomore Production Lab will use the knowledge and skills they have picked up over their first three semesters to create a short original animated film or game project. They will work individually or collaboratively to execute their creative vision while learning to budget their time as they navigate their way through a full production pipeline. Major requirement: 3 cr./semester; 6 Hrs/week.

title: Major Capstone Production.  (3 cr. or 6 cr. option)
description: Using the project started in AG 421 Major Capstone Project Pre-Production, students create a final animation, game, or collection of visual development assets to be presented in the BFA Thesis Show. Additionally, students will give a public lecture on their Capstone Production and supply appropriate documentation of the project for the online BFA Thesis Show. All work will be included in the portfolio developed in SEM 451 Professional Studio. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week. 6 credit option.

title: Character Animation
description: Key poses, gestures, and timing are just a few of the principles that animators use to breathe life into characters. Animators also apply subtle or overt acting cues to convey thought and emotion through their characters. In this class, we deeply explore personality and emotion as students generate compelling performances in their medium of choice (2D, 3D, Stop-motion).

title: Introduction to Drawing
description: This course introduces drawing from perception, including techniques for measurement, contour, massing, volume, and rendering illumination through value. Compositional skills such as placement and figure/ground interaction are stressed.  Drawing materials include vine charcoal, compressed charcoal, stamp, and india ink; subject matter includes still life, interior, landscape, and the figure.

title: Introduction to Drawing
description: This course introduces drawing from perception, including techniques for measurement, contour, massing, volume, and rendering illumination through value. Compositional skills such as placement and figure/ground interaction are stressed.  Drawing materials include vine charcoal, compressed charcoal, stamp, and india ink; subject matter includes still life, interior, landscape, and the figure.

title: CURViture
description: This course examines the conceptual and structural possibilities of bending wood as it applies to functional and sculptural approaches. Assignments and exercises focus on the practice and practical application of solid wood and plywood. Exercises will cover simple and complex bends, fixture mechanics, and clamp/vacuum bag applications. Various bending methods will include brick-laying, lamination and steam. CNC technology may also be used to augment these techniques. Students will apply these newly acquired skills to make a minimum of one project.

title: Introduction to Woodworking
description: This is a beginning level introduction to wood as a visual and building material. Assignments and exercises focus on the application of various techniques such as carving and fabrication to create dynamic forms that explore function & composition. The use of power and hand tools will be applied to create a variety of objects that illustrate an in-depth understanding of working with wood. Students will first design and create custom croquet mallets, utilizing largely reductive methods, which will be used in an outdoor croquet match. A small Shaker style end table will be made as an instructional exercise leading into a final custom occasional table design and build.

title: Models & Mockups
description: Working with wood is often expensive, time consuming and requires a great deal of planning. This course will explore that crucial planning process as we focus on model making, scaled samples and full-size mockups. Through the use of appropriate techniques and tools from nail guns to the laser cutter, we will build sets, models and examples of larger work proposals. Assignments will be individually tailored to fit your studio work interests and cover topics related to achieving your own goals within your studio practice, such as bringing your digital environmental work into the real world.

title: Wood & Furniture Major Studio
description: The completion of Junior Studio is dedicated toward learning intermediate to advanced techniques as well as the development of a strong understanding of wood as a building material. Individual projects will allow for exploration in concept, utility and technique. The primary mode of making will be in the Krenovian Standing Cabinet. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Wood & Furniture Major Studio
description: Students are given quick warm-up assignments throughout the year while concurrently developing their work in close communication with faculty. This course is predominantly dedicated toward the development of the students’ body of work that will accompany their written thesis. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Major Capstone Project Post-production
description: This course is an extension of AG 422 in which students produce a significant Thesis or Capstone project. Examples of these projects include: Short films, Animations, 2D and 3D Games or Game Art, Interactive Media Installations, Collections of Concept Art with animatics and/or promotional trailers. Time in this course is devoted to project production and post-production including the production of written and visual promotional materials. Students may choose to write an optional research paper on their final project if they feel that research and writing supports and extends their studio practice. Major requirement: 6 credits/semester; 3 hours/week.

title: Ceramics Throwing-Advanced
description: In this ceramics course, the student will build upon throwing skills developed in CE 201 and/or other existing throwing skills. Complex forms are studied including pouring vessels, lidded containers, plates, platters, and teapots, Techniques of creating handles, knobs, and other appendages will be studied.  Students will develop skills necessary to make larger forms by stacking and joining thrown sections.   Students will learn to establish relationships between functional objects in a set, and will explore the creative potential of altering and manipulating thrown forms. Exploration of surface decorating techniques and glaze solutions will be included. Stoneware will be used predominantly with an option for use of porcelain.

title: Illustration Majors Studio
description: This course is devoted to an instructor-facilitated independent project (thesis). Students will create professional level illustrations and establish a body of work for portfolio presentation and for inclusion in the senior thesis BFA display. A written and supported component to the independent project is required. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.
