The class explores these principles through developing an understanding of the body as a dynamic object in time and space, lens-based seeing, light, – 9 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] audio, sequential and process-based drawing, and the moving image. Processes and materials include both conventional and digital approaches. The course blends multiple working methodologies, strategies and tools to emphasize the specific learning objectives. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Experimental Photography
description: This course will explore the malleable and plural ways artists engage with the photographic medium. The assignments and coursework will encourage students to incorporate an interdisciplinary approach to the medium. We will cover present and past artists who have used photography as an extension of their studio practice and have had an experimental approach to the medium. Students will be expected to think outside the box and utilize new methods for creating photographic work. Lectures, group – 19 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] discussions, and assignments will cover photography, silkscreen, collages, artist books, and sculptural considerations.

title: Senior Synthesis
description: This course is taken in the final semester of the senior year. It is an integral course with the studio practice. Students will be led through a guided research and writing process to identify and explore what their inspirations are for their studio work, how those interests are played out historically and what their relevance is to contemporary art and culture. The class will culminate in a fully articulated written thesis that explains and details their thesis work. Students will present their ideas at various points throughout the semester and conversations will be held about the ideas underlying their work both in the classroom and in individualized studio visits. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 3 hours/week.

title: Storyboarding for Animation
description: In animation, storytelling is about much more than writing a script. This course will explore the full story development pipeline from loglines and storybeats to finished animatic. We will cover the basics of narrative structure, cinematic grammar, and story-based character development with a specific focus on developing narrative or experimental work for animation.

title: Critical Approaches to Contemporary Art
description: This class provides a foundation in critical theory and in critical thinking and writing skills. We will consider the relationship between the theory and practice of art. Each week we will look at a different critical issue related to making and interpreting art, covering- the sometimes overlapping- issues of form, process, representation, reproduction, originality, distribution, institutions, gender, identity, culture and politics. We will frame these issues in relation to specific case studies drawn from a range of media, including examples from studio areas at MECA&D. Students will be encouraged to make links between critical issues covered in class and their own work, and to understand the ways that theory connects to artistic practice.

title: Critical Approaches to Contemporary Art
description: This class provides a foundation in critical theory and in critical thinking and writing skills. We will consider the relationship between the theory and practice of art. Each week we will look at a different critical issue related to making and interpreting art, covering- the sometimes overlapping- issues of form, process, representation, reproduction, originality, distribution, institutions, gender, identity, culture and politics. We will frame these issues in relation to specific case studies drawn from a range of media, including examples from studio areas at MECA&D. Students will be encouraged to make links between critical issues covered in class and their own work, and to understand the ways that theory connects to artistic practice.

title: (AE) The Business of Art
description: The practical and administrative aspects of a profession and life in the Arts (visual, fiber, graphic and other) are examined in this seminar. Topics include: speaking articulately about your work, budgeting basics, marketing your work, building a sustainable fundraising program that fits your artistic practice, cultivating relationships with key industry professionals, pitching your artistry, among other topics. Guest Artists and Arts Administrators from New England will join us with informative presentations that shed light on the practicalities and realities of being a professional artist. Over the course of the semester, students will design a hands- on plan and accompanying budget focused on developing and pursuing a life in the arts post-graduation.  By the end of the course, participants will be better prepared to perform the business tasks expected of arts professionals aimed at promoting your work, funding it and growing artistically within the larger arts landscape.

title: The Book as Visual Object
description: This rigorous, in-depth examination of the book as an art form will introduce students to traditional bookbinding materials, structures and techniques. Regular assignments or "prompts" will present an opportunity to apply and translate those structures incorporating them into one's own studio practice. Emphasis will be on encouraging participants to tailor what is presented in class to suit their own needs and aesthetic and to explore the endless possibilities of the book. The semester will be comprised of weekly hands-on demonstrations, guided studio time, critiques and periodic field trips.

title: 4D: Space & Temporality
description: This course investigates modes of practice beyond the creation of objects. Space and Temporality focuses on verbs; process as opposed to product. Space & Temporality cultivates an understanding of design and composition within time and space. The class explores these principles through developing an understanding of the body as a dynamic object in time and space, lens-based seeing, light, – 9 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] audio, sequential and process-based drawing, and the moving image. Processes and materials include both conventional and digital approaches. The course blends multiple working methodologies, strategies and tools to emphasize the specific learning objectives. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Large Format Photography
description: Working with a view camera, students explore the unique aesthetic possibilities of the large negative. The zone system of exposure control, fine printing techniques, chemical formulation, archival processing and methods of presentation are also covered.

title: Introduction to Photography
description: Students are introduced to the vocabulary of photography through a series of projects designed to increase their perceptual skills. Emphasis is on exploring the world with a camera in order to understand the unique properties of photographic form and then to learn how to use these properties for communication and self-expression. Exposure, film, print development and analogue darkroom skills are the basis for this class.

title: Gender Theory
description: Although theoretical reflection on the nature and meaning of gender is perhaps as old as theory itself, recent innovations in the philosophical study of gender have changed the way we think, speak and act.  At the same time, however, many of the critical concepts and theoretical frameworks mobilized by gender theorists have been sources of significant controversy both inside and outside the academy.  This course introduces the philosophical study of gender, paying close attention to recent developments and debates within the field.

title: English Composition
description: Reading and writing are essential parts of college learning and successful art & design practice. In this class, students will explore a variety of approaches to reading and writing, including close reading and textual analysis, critical and comparative analysis, and reflective, argumentative writing. Students will be introduced to three major genres of text: the novel; the graphic novel; and poetry.

title: English Composition
description: Reading and writing are essential parts of college learning and successful art & design practice. In this class, students will explore a variety of approaches to reading and writing, including close reading and textual analysis, critical and comparative analysis, and reflective, argumentative writing. Students will be introduced to three major genres of text: the novel; the graphic novel; and poetry.

title: Zombie Poiesis: Beyond Capitalism
description: In “Theses on a Philosophy of History,” Walter Benjamin emphasizes the importance of “the ancestors” to the struggle against fascism. This course will examine the figure of the zombie as a generative ally in navigating the end of a colonial capitalist modernity built on racial violence. Grounding ourselves in a critical history of the zombie as an aspect of nineteenth-century Afro-Caribbean religious liberation practices, we will explore approaches contemporary thinkers and makers have taken in incorporating the dead as living part of their practices. We will also consider the role of the dead in our own practices.

title: Special Topics: Post Production & VFX for Animation
description: Every animation and film production follows a production pipeline that ends in post. This course will cover the processes and tools of post-production. With an emphasis on compositing, visual effects, motion tracking, keying, transitions, track mattes, motion graphics and more.  Students will gain an understanding of the technical pipeline for digital assets and use of the tools of Post production to polish and finish their work. They will work to combine various media and styles of work from Stop motion to 2D/3D animation and live action elements. Students are welcome to bring assets from their current projects to work with in class.

title: Collaborative Productions
description: Teams of students work with faculty mentors to create and produce short animations or games. Emphasis is placed on individual engagement, creative vision, open communication, and setting goals. Projects will move from the writer’s table, through pre- production to production, and on to post-production. All team members will contribute ideas, support, and labor in each step of the process. Alliances and teams may choose to work collaboratively in other Animation and Game Art courses. Major requirement: 3 credits/ semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Intro to 3D Modeling & Animation
description: Great characters don’t happen by accident—they are the result of deliberate, thoughtful design. In this course, we will analyze iconic characters from games and animation, breaking down what makes them memorable and effective. Through a progressive, iterative process, students will develop their own characters with a focus on visual impact, audience relevance, and personality-driven design. We will emphasize structured iteration, exploring how shape, proportion, silhouette, and detail work together to strengthen a character’s identity. Additionally, students will refine their designs through backstory development, prop exploration, and stylization. By the end of the course, students will have fully realized character concepts ready for entertainment design.

title: Major Production Studio II
description: Students continue explorations begun in AG 321 or start new experiments in animation or game art. Faculty will determine the depth and number of projects appropriate for students on an individual basis. Mandatory group critiques serve as anchor points in project scheduling. All projects will result in finished work for inclusion in a portfolio to be developed in SEM 451 Professional Studio. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Major Production Studio II
description: Students continue explorations begun in AG 321 or start new experiments in animation or game art. Faculty will determine the depth and number of projects appropriate for students on an individual basis. Mandatory group critiques serve as anchor points in project scheduling. All projects will result in finished work for inclusion in a portfolio to be developed in SEM 451 Professional Studio. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Special Topics: Storyboarding II
description: This course will cover more advanced topics in storyboarding, such as shot selection, framing, pacing, camera movement and editing animatics. Weekly assignments will emphasize refining their boards based on regular reviews and peer feedback. The goal will be to simulate a studio environment where addressing notes and hitting deadlines are expected.

title: Animation & Game Art Concepts and History
description: This course offers a historical and critical overview of animation and game art. Case studies in animation and game art will be presented and discussed. Assigned readings, playings, and viewings will introduce and illustrate the formal, conceptual, and kinetic norms of animation and narrative across media platforms. Students will create written and/or visual responses to each of the major themes of the course. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Songwriting
description: This course will focus on individual creativity as well as collaboration. Students will be given writing assignments and explore in-class brainstorming sessions. This includes writing as a band/group and establishing musical partnerships that divide responsibilities, e.g. lyrics and music. The class will include creation and performance of music. Basic music theory concepts will be introduced to support and enhance the creative process.

title: Instrumental Lab: Choral Ensemble  1 cr. class runs 7-week (1/11-2/26)
description: If you like singing in a group or want to learn, this class is for you. Students will learn to sing in a group setting and as a featured soloist. Contemporary and traditional vocal music will be performed. No previous experience needed for this 7-week course.

title: Illustration in Context
description: An illustration is generally commissioned to convey and promote a particular idea and/or product. This course is an introduction to illustration in a professional context, balancing an emphasis on concept and individual expression with continuous development of skills. Students will be exposed to different uses of illustration within the contexts of editorial, book, advertising, and product illustration. Class projects will emphasize the role of illustration in communication, the play between idea and text, with a highlight on process. Departmental Requirement for majors: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Editorial Illustration: Concept to Print
description: This class provides a comprehensive, step-by-step guide to the modern editorial illustration process, designed to assist students transform the written word into compelling visual narratives while fostering a personal artistic voice. This structured creative process includes article analysis, ideation, revisions, production, and finalized illustrations. Personal point of view, visual metaphor, experimentation, and exploration of materials will be encouraged. Evaluation of the current editorial industry and editorial illustrators will be discussed and examined.

title: Illustration Majors Studio
description: This course is devoted to an instructor-facilitated independent project (thesis). Students will create professional level illustrations and establish a body of work for portfolio presentation and for inclusion in the senior thesis BFA display. A written and supported component to the independent project is required. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Art History Survey II
description: The second semester of the art history survey course combines a chronological overview of art works from the seventeenth century through up to 1960 with a rigorous investigation of the contexts in which art has been made. The course covers major art historical developments in the western and non-western worlds with an evaluation of their form, content, style, cultural meaning, and historical import. The course also introduces students to art historical vocabulary and various methods of art historical research. Each section meets twice a week for lecture and student discussion.

title: Art History Survey II
description: The second semester of the art history survey course combines a chronological overview of art works from the seventeenth century through up to 1960 with a rigorous investigation of the contexts in which art has been made. The course covers major art historical developments in the western and non-western worlds with an evaluation of their form, content, style, cultural meaning, and historical import. The course also introduces students to art historical vocabulary and various methods of art historical research. Each section meets twice a week for lecture and student discussion.

title: 19th-Century Media Worlds
description: This course explores some of the nineteenth-century media that shaped the art and visual culture of global modernity and, in numerous cases, that of other disciplinary spheres. Whether newly invented, ‘revived,’ or of longer standing, media including wood engravings, electrotypes, daguerreotypes, plaster casts, chromolithographs, woodblock prints, glass, and embroidery participated in local and global circuits of exchange, changing how people looked at and understood the world and themselves. Students will learn about diverse mediums’ material dimensions, movements, and legacies, as well as their adopters’ and users’ negotiations of invention and tradition.

title: (W) Art History Minor Thesis
description: Students pursuing the minor may enroll in this course either semester. Students work with a thesis advisor. Independent Study: 3 credits/semester; 3 hours/week.

title: Paintings without Pictures
description: This course is a great cross-disciplinary experience: 2D, 3D and 4D artists benefit from exploring an alternative visual vocabulary. It pushes away from external references and allows artists to explore a variety of ways to express themselves. This can include emotional expression, decorative embellishment, complex metaphors, spiritual connections, material explorations, and philosophical inquiry. This class examines the persistence of the visual art object, with particular emphasis on abstract painting. Abstraction offers exciting opportunities for artists. A series of exercises will provide the opportunity to experiment with traditions of abstract imagery, materials, and processes in an effort to help them develop a vocabulary of their own. A series of visual presentations will accompany assignments, and periodic field trips will support the content of the class work. This course will also continue to build on the formal skills gathered from Foundation Painting, including color development, drawing and compositional skills, and verbal analysis of form.

title: Painting Majors Studio. (3-6 cr option w/Chair permission)
description: This course provides an uninterrupted opportunity for the advanced painter to develop independently. Instructors critique, lecture, give demonstrations, and participate with students in both one-on-one situations and group discussions. Emphasis is placed on developing a personal painting process, while building and sustaining an extended body of work. The course also addresses the realities of being a working artist in contemporary culture (i.e. gallery representation, grants & funding, job information, etc.). Visiting artists, trips to museums and galleries, readings and lectures help to facilitate these goals. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester, 6-credit possibility with permission of the Chair; 6 hours/week.

title: Forms and Transformation
description: This course is a great cross-disciplinary experience: 2D, 3D and 4D artists benefit from exploring an alternative visual vocabulary. It pushes away from external references and allows artists to explore a variety of ways to express themselves. This can include emotional expression, decorative embellishment, complex metaphors, spiritual connections, material explorations, and philosophical inquiry. This class examines the persistence of the visual art object, with particular emphasis on abstract painting. Abstraction offers exciting opportunities for artists. A series of exercises will provide the opportunity to experiment with traditions of abstract imagery, materials, and processes in an effort to help them develop a vocabulary of their own. A series of visual presentations will accompany assignments, and periodic field trips will support the content of the class work. This course will also continue to build on the formal skills gathered from Foundation Painting, including color development, drawing and compositional skills, and verbal analysis of form.

title: Character Design for Games & Animation
description: Great characters don’t happen by accident—they are the result of deliberate, thoughtful design. In this course, we will analyze iconic characters from games and animation, breaking down what makes them memorable and effective. Through a progressive, iterative process, students will develop their own characters with a focus on visual impact, audience relevance, and personality-driven design. We will emphasize structured iteration, exploring how shape, proportion, silhouette, and detail work together to strengthen a character’s identity. Additionally, students will refine their designs through backstory development, prop exploration, and stylization. By the end of the course, students will have fully realized character concepts ready for entertainment design.

title: Draping & Construction
description: By draping directly on the dressform, students develop sloper patterns for foundation garment shapes. Slopers are then modified through pattern development to create basic styles. Students study correct draping technique, basic pattern drafting skills, and professional pattern marking and annotation. Throughout the course, students study garment construction, finishing techniques, and fit assessment through creating both sample garments and finished garments. Projects provide opportunities to apply draping and drafting skills to develop unique garments that investigate the expressive potential of silhouette, color, texture and materials to convey concepts. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Printmaking 1: Relief
description: Students gain a working understanding of the tools, materials, and processes of relief printmaking. Through intensive exploration of several techniques, including linocut, woodcut, and multi color printing, students develop and refine personal images. Students will gain familiarity with safe and professional printmaking practices and develop a language for printmaking critique. This is a great studio class to explore your existing ideas related to drawing, painting, design and illustration.

title: Printmaking Majors Studio
description: This course emphasizes the development and enhancement of each student’s studio practice and the production of a range of work in a range of media. The primary focus is on your work, looking specifically at how you articulate your ideas, the quality and quantity of your work and your contextualization of what you make. The course structure provides a framework on which students can build an increasingly personalized studio exploration. Assignments leave plenty of room for the assertion of self and guide students through generative and iterative processes designed to encourage experimentation, risk taking, and the deployment of already established passions as well as movement into unknown territories. Student work is refined through a rigorous critique process and individual studio meetings with faculty. Moving through a range of projects, students build skills and acquire the knowledge to critically position their work within the larger historical and conceptual framework that defines the increasingly broad field of print. Studio assignments, technical demonstrations, readings, research presentations, critique, field trips, workshops and special projects all present students with new ideas and ways of making studio work and crafting a sustainable practice, while also helping students increase their communication and critical thinking skills. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Printmaking Majors Studio
description: This course emphasizes the development and enhancement of your studio practice and the production of a cohesive body of work. The primary focus is on your work, looking specifically at how you articulate your ideas, the quality and quantity of your work and your contextualization of what you make. Seniors work in a self-directed manner in terms of media and content, with deadlines for in progress and final critiques propelling the student toward the completion of a fully realized Thesis. Student work is refined through a rigorous critique process and individual studio meetings with faculty. Students will critically position their work within the larger historical and conceptual framework that defines the increasingly broad field of print. Studio projects, technical demonstrations, readings, research presentations, critique, field trips, workshops and special projects offer students further ideas and ways of making studio work and crafting a sustainable practice, while also helping students increase their communication and critical thinking skills. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: (S) Surface Design
description: Surface design focuses on the creation of color and pattern on textile surfaces through dyeing, embroidery, and surface manipulation. Students create technical samples and also critically explore the role of textile surfaces in sustainability including learning to apply surface design techniques to alter and redesign post-consumer materials into new textiles through mending, stitching, and dyeing. Through the creation of a color swatch library, students develop a technical understanding of natural dyes, dye chemistry, and the inherent strengths and weaknesses of working with various dye materials: synthetic dyes, fresh plant matter, raw dried materials, dried ground materials, and natural extracts. With an understanding of dye chemistry in place, students will further alter textile surfaces through the application of pattern through mending, piecing, embroidery, and surface manipulation.

title: Collection Development
description: Focusing on creative development and technical growth, students develop a body of work that is conceptually cohesive and learn to apply technical skill and craftsmanship to professional output. Students practice how to sketch, refine, and develop collections of work and how to apply research concepts to studio output. Throughout the semester, students develop a body of work from concept to finished work through a process of creating technical samples, sourcing materials, developing designs, assessing design success, and constructing finished pieces. This course emphasizes the development of professional skills, personal organization, and – 22 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] time management related to complex projects. Students practice professional presentation techniques at the end of the semester by exhibiting their finished body of work either in an exhibition or as part of the Fashion Show and developing a portfolio of documentation. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Branding and Visual Systems
description: This course is an exploration of visual identity and systems through historical, formal, and conceptual perspectives. Students will develop original logos, patterns, palettes, series, and typographic systems. They will practice working within constraints, designing applications at various scales and media, and codifying their design systems into comprehensive style guides. Graphic Design Major requirement.

title: Intermediate Metalsmithing I + Advanced Metalsmithing I
Metalsmithing & Jewelry Majors Studio
description: This one-year class introduces students to the full breadth of the studio practice of the Metalsmithing and Jewelry major. Students build on their previous skills while learning new technical skills. Students will develop a solid conceptual base for their work and – 17 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] discover modes of working and interests that are specific to them through research, drawing and model making as well as finished pieces. Incorporated into the curriculum are projects that challenge students both conceptually and technically. In addition to assignments, self-designed explorations of technical, conceptual or design directions are included. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Metalsmithing & Jewelry Majors Studio
description: The final year provides students with the opportunity to work in an independent manner. New technical skills may be introduced, yet emphasis is placed on honing already acquired skills. Each student is expected to select, research, and articulate a direction that culminates into a related body of work and written thesis. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Color Photography
description: Color Photography introduces students to the concepts of the digital darkroom. Time will be spent studying the canon of color photography, and the artists using color materials as a primary means of expression. The primary goal of the class is to create a portfolio of exhibition quality inkjet prints. Skill sets covered but not limited to: scanning of color films, use of digital cameras, image manipulation software, color management and calibration tools.

title: Introduction to the Discipline
description: This seminar course provides an overview of the history of illustration from the perspective of critical genres and movements in illustration. Students will participate in lectures, engage in readings, give presentations, and will work on projects individually and collaboratively. Students engage in a dialogue of the uses, definitions, and value of illustration as an enduring social art form. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Metalsmithing & Jewelry I
description: This one-semester introductory course blends technical instruction with an investigation of design and concept as it relates to ornament and function. Students develop confidence and proficiency with the basic skills of forming, soldering, and finishing. Classroom discussions and image presentations introduce the broad range of possibilities that the field of Metalsmithing and Jewelry encompasses that are part of this diverse and exciting field.

title: 3D Materiality: Metalsmithing 7-week class runs 1/11-2/26
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: 3D Materiality: Metalsmithing 7-week class runs 3/1-4/23
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: English Composition
description: Reading and writing are essential parts of college learning and successful art & design practice. In this class, students will explore a variety of approaches to reading and writing, including close reading and textual analysis, critical and comparative analysis, and reflective, argumentative writing. Students will be introduced to three major genres of text: the novel; the graphic novel; and poetry.

title: English Composition
description: Reading and writing are essential parts of college learning and successful art & design practice. In this class, students will explore a variety of approaches to reading and writing, including close reading and textual analysis, critical and comparative analysis, and reflective, argumentative writing. Students will be introduced to three major genres of text: the novel; the graphic novel; and poetry.

title: Slavery, Abolition, and the coming of Civil War
description: Ours are most certainly fractured and fraught times. It has been said of the US that we are in the midst of a cold civil war. Too, folks ask, Has it—the country, the world, etc.—ever been this bad? Well, yes—at least as pertains to the country. In the 19th century a very hot civil war blazed for four years, killing some 700,000 souls by latest estimate, and coming very near, in 1863, to bringing the curtain down on American democracy. Now seems an instructive moment to return to the antebellum 19th century to (re)discover how and why the nation barreled toward war. Some reasons will undoubtedly feel unfamiliar. Others, however, including the seizure of Black and brown folk by federal agents, the demonizing of one's political opposition, and questions over who is and who is not a citizen, most certainly will not. History has much to tell us about our current moment, this history—the years leading to the Civil War—particularly so given the times in which we live.

title: (PE) Empty Bowls & Community Engagement
description: This course offers students a practical and experiential learning experience in community engagement by focusing on organizing and executing an "Empty Bowls" fundraiser.  One of the critical aspects of the course involves creating ceramic bowls. Students will learn the art of vessel making and ceramics, exploring different techniques to shape and mold clay into beautiful and functional bowls. This hands-on experience will not only develop their artistic abilities but also instill a sense of craftsmanship and creativity. Students will also be involved in sourcing food from local commercial kitchens. They will learn about the importance of food safety, quality, and sustainability while establishing connections with local businesses. This process will provide insights into the logistics of acquiring food resources for the fundraiser and the significance of supporting local communities. The course emphasizes the establishment of partnerships with organizations that combat food insecurity. Students will learn about the challenges faced by individuals and communities affected by food insecurity and explore ways to collaborate with relevant organizations. This aspect of the course fosters a deeper understanding of the social issues surrounding food security and the importance of community support. Marketing plays a crucial role in the success of any fundraising event, and this course recognizes that. Students will learn about effective marketing strategies and techniques to promote the "Empty Bowls" fundraiser. They will gain practical experience in creating promotional materials, utilizing social media platforms, and engaging with the community to raise awareness and generate interest in the event. The culmination of the course is a ticketed event where the bowls created by the students will be showcased and sold to raise funds for local food security initiatives.Students will actively participate in organizing and managing this event, gaining valuable event planning and coordination skills. This hands-on experience will provide a real-world understanding of the efforts required to execute a successful fundraising event. Overall, this course offers students a comprehensive and immersive learning experience in community engagement. By actively participating in the various stages of the "Empty Bowls" fundraiser, students will develop practical skills, gain a deeper understanding of food security issues, and positively impact their local community. ”

title: 4D: Space & Temporality
description: This course investigates modes of practice beyond the creation of objects. Space and Temporality focuses on verbs; process as opposed to product. Space & Temporality cultivates an understanding of design and composition within time and space. The class explores these principles through developing an understanding of the body as a dynamic object in time and space, lens-based seeing, light, – 9 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] audio, sequential and process-based drawing, and the moving image. Processes and materials include both conventional and digital approaches. The course blends multiple working methodologies, strategies and tools to emphasize the specific learning objectives. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: The Sculptural Imagination: Wood, Steel and Foundry Practices
description: This course is an introduction to the basic techniques involved in woodworking, foundry processes, and welded steel sculpture. We will investigate materials, methods, and concepts that serve as the foundation for creating sculptural forms. Through weekly assignments, lectures, demonstrations, and discussions we will explore traditional and experimental approaches to the theory and practice involved in making sculpture. In each of the assignments we will study the historical, cultural, and contemporary applications and implications of these given sculptural techniques, however, the subject matter for all assignments is open to your individual interpretation. Students will learn how to identify materials, proper material usage, learn tools and safety within the Sculpture area, and learn articulation of these materials (both physically and audibly) via critiques of finished works. The primary objective for this course is to develop the skills needed to interpret and articulate contemporary modes of creating a sculpture that students will encounter as professional artists and creative problem solvers.

title: Sculpture: Senior Major Studio. (3-6 cr option)
description: This course equips students with the wherewithal to sustain artistic growth and emphasizes an independent and significant investigation of personal artistic interests and sensibilities. Students determine the nature of the work they produce, choosing technical means, materials, scale and context. Sculpture is approached as the perceptual and philosophical exploration of form and our interactions with it. Visual organization and craftsmanship, essential to making clear content, are stressed. Two complete works and supporting material in the form of three dimensional studies or experiments and drawings are expected, culminating in the presentation of a written thesis. Students work in all studios of the department. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Senior Synthesis
description: This course is taken in the final semester of the senior year. It is an integral course with the studio practice. Students will be led through a guided research and writing process to identify and explore what their inspirations are for their studio work, how those interests are played out historically and what their relevance is to contemporary art and culture. The class will culminate in a fully articulated written thesis that explains and details their thesis work. Students will present their ideas at various points throughout the semester and conversations will be held about the ideas underlying their work both in the classroom and in individualized studio visits. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 3 hours/week.

title: Independent Projects in Painting
description: This course is designed to help students learn to create structured parameters for independent work. Students working in any discipline will develop and investigate their ideas through the lens of painting.  Students/instructor discussions will establish self directed projects throughout the semester.  Emphasis will be placed on form as it relates to content, research and process.  Specific materials will be determined by the nature of the independent project.

title: Major Capstone Project Post-production
description: This course is an extension of AG 422 in which students produce a significant Thesis or Capstone project. Examples of these projects include: Short films, Animations, 2D and 3D Games or Game Art, Interactive Media Installations, Collections of Concept Art with animatics and/or promotional trailers. Time in this course is devoted to project production and post-production including the production of written and visual promotional materials. Students may choose to write an optional research paper on their final project if they feel that research and writing supports and extends their studio practice. Major requirement: 6 credits/semester; 3 hours/week.

title: Weaving
description: Students gain a comprehensive understanding of weaving using floor looms while exploring compositional strategies of woven textile design. The course emphasizes the expressive potential of color, texture, fiber, pattern, yarn, and surface in combination with woven structures. Students study yarn handling, loom mechanics, color and weave combination, and categories of woven structures while creating an index of woven samples and a series of fully finished woven projects.

title: Majors Thesis Studio (6 cr.)
description: Seniors investigate areas of specific interest in textiles or fashion in order to realize a senior thesis based on a comprehensively researched personal concept that synthesizes references in order to develop new knowledge. Students work through processes of ideation, sourcing, prototyping, and finishing their chosen body of work. Emphasis is placed on professional practices, time management, and the development of a sustainable studio practice while executing thesis work. Learning takes place through group discussions, shared critiques, and studio visits. Major requirement: 6 credits/semester; 12 hours/week.

title: Advanced Drawing- (Capstone students only)
description: This course concentrates on creating a structured environment in which students can begin to build independent working processes and linear bodies of work. It is designed to complement the experience in the major studio, despite the student's chosen area of concentration. Emphasis is placed on developing content driven work and research skills, while continuing to focus on the formal aspects of a drawing experience. Course serves as the capstone experience for the Drawing Minor.

title: Illustration in Context
description: An illustration is generally commissioned to convey and promote a particular idea and/or product. This course is an introduction to illustration in a professional context, balancing an emphasis on concept and individual expression with continuous development of skills. Students will be exposed to different uses of illustration within the contexts of editorial, book, advertising, and product illustration. Class projects will emphasize the role of illustration in communication, the play between idea and text, with a highlight on process. Departmental Requirement for majors: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Illustration Majors Studio
description: This course introduces students to new visual problems, illustration formats, and image making solutions. Students will develop original concepts built on research and observation as well as conceptual ideation and invention. Those concepts and ideas will be resolved while refining manual drawing skills and exploring various techniques for creating finished illustrations. This course will be based around various relevant assignments that explore both the expressive and representational nature of illustration, accompanied by discussion of contemporary illustration practices. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Advanced Comics
description: Do you have an idea for a graphic novel or longform visual narrative?In this course, students will apply skills from Introduction to Comics to focus on one longer project throughout the semester, such as a 32-page comic or an equivalent excerpt of a graphic novel. Students will have the opportunity to brainstorm ideas, and advance their skills in research, scriptwriting, book design, and visual production. Alongside their semester-long projects, students will read and analyze content from well-known cartoonists, and develop storytelling skills that allow them to empathize and connect with readers on an emotional level. Students will be encouraged to take risks and experiment with narrative techniques, visual styles, media, and form. They will enhance their understanding of the medium through visits to exhibitions and public libraries, presentations with local artists, discussions with publishers, and through regular critiques with their professor and peers. By the end of the course, students can potentially self-publish their comic or pitch their graphic novel project to a publisher or literary agent.

title: Critical Approaches to Contemporary Art
description: This class provides a foundation in critical theory and in critical thinking and writing skills. We will consider the relationship between the theory and practice of art. Each week we will look at a different critical issue related to making and interpreting art, covering- the sometimes overlapping- issues of form, process, representation, reproduction, originality, distribution, institutions, gender, identity, culture and politics. We will frame these issues in relation to specific case studies drawn from a range of media, including examples from studio areas at MECA&D. Students will be encouraged to make links between critical issues covered in class and their own work, and to understand the ways that theory connects to artistic practice.

title: Critical Approaches to Contemporary Art
description: This class provides a foundation in critical theory and in critical thinking and writing skills. We will consider the relationship between the theory and practice of art. Each week we will look at a different critical issue related to making and interpreting art, covering- the sometimes overlapping- issues of form, process, representation, reproduction, originality, distribution, institutions, gender, identity, culture and politics. We will frame these issues in relation to specific case studies drawn from a range of media, including examples from studio areas at MECA&D. Students will be encouraged to make links between critical issues covered in class and their own work, and to understand the ways that theory connects to artistic practice.

title: Contemporary Practices for Art Teaching
description: This course introduces undergraduates to the core principles and frameworks for teaching and learning art techniques. Students will develop an understanding of current instructional practices and gain confidence teaching children through guided practice. Rooted in both theory and practice, this hybrid philosophy and studio course teaches students to apply the dynamics of group inquiry while also making art. Direct experimentation with pedagogy, materials, and art-making are key components in this course. Students will plan and implement meaningful learning activities with community partners, such as museums, schools, or community centers.

title: RELEASE Outreach Director Course: Student Teacher Observations

description: This course investigates modes of practice beyond the creation of objects. Space and Temporality focuses on verbs; process as opposed to product. Space & Temporality cultivates an understanding of design and composition within time and space. The class explores these principles through developing an understanding of the body as a dynamic object in time and space, lens-based seeing, light, – 9 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] audio, sequential and process-based drawing, and the moving image. Processes and materials include both conventional and digital approaches. The course blends multiple working methodologies, strategies and tools to emphasize the specific learning objectives. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Club as Context: Collage, Sound, and Space
notes: Fast Track - only running next year
description: Building off of concepts and exercises explored in Space and Temporality, this course situates DJing and the nightclub as a form of social sculpture, installation, sound collage that invites embodiment. Students will engage with the historical and cultural roots of DJ culture while developing technical and creative skills in DJing and sound editing seen through a sculptural lens. By drawing connections between sculpture and sound, students will create projects that range from Audio Collages, installations, movement exercises and group planning that consider how music and space interact, transforming environments and shaping social experiences. An emphasis on figurative sculpture, installation, sound and movement will are be present in this course.

title: Artist, Museums & the Art Market
description: This course surveys the vibrant world of global contemporary art-presentation, distribution and making. Combining lectures, guest speakers, and regular field trips to museums, galleries, artists' studios and private collections in Portland, Maine and New England, this course will present students with an overview of recent developments and important directions in new art and the institutions and individuals that collect and present it. In weekly readings about and discussions of the practices, possibilities and politics of the art world, a recurring theme will be critical thinking and career strategies for professional artists. Students will be required to read interpretive texts and to write several short "Impression Paper" essays on course subjects as well as develop a major presentation on a course theme of their choosing. Active participation in classroom discussions and meetings with guest speakers is required and a factor in grading. Please note that due to the field trip-intensive nature of this course and the logistics of scheduling meetings with curators, artists, and others, the class schedule is subject to change.

title: Studio in Context: Approaches to Critical Theory
Collaborative Productions
description: Teams of students work with faculty mentors to create and produce short animations or games. Emphasis is placed on individual engagement, creative vision, open communication, and setting goals. Projects will move from the writer’s table, through pre- production to production, and on to post-production. All team members will contribute ideas, support, and labor in each step of the process. Alliances and teams may choose to work collaboratively in other Animation and Game Art courses. Major requirement: 3 credits/ semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Major Capstone Production.  (3 cr. or 6 cr. option)
description: Using the project started in AG 421 Major Capstone Project Pre-Production, students create a final animation, game, or collection of visual development assets to be presented in the BFA Thesis Show. Additionally, students will give a public lecture on their Capstone Production and supply appropriate documentation of the project for the online BFA Thesis Show. All work will be included in the portfolio developed in SEM 451 Professional Studio. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week. 6 credit option.

title: (W) Creative Writing
description: While visual artists work with material like paint, paper, pigment, stone, metal, fabric, and ink, the writer’s primary material is language. In this class, we will explore language as a creative medium with close attention to the ways in which language already informs – or can inform –  artistic practice. Seeking both to enhance established modes and develop new possibilities, we will consider the ways in which writing can help us cultivate receptivity to inspiration, forge connections to other artists, and fruitfully revise our work. In this class, you will be invited to experiment with creative writing as an art form and a medium of communication, reflection, and revision that can embolden and animate your visual practice.

title: (S) Livable Worlds
description: Whether under political occupation, psychic pressure, or environmental duress artists, writers and filmmakers have found ways to live in what are otherwise unlivable conditions. In this class, we will focus on fantasy-worlds in dreams and drawings; imaginations of political community through deep listening and sonic haunting; the intersection of resistance with gendered experience; and how environmental art has occupied public space in liberatory ways. How can surrealist topographical maps, site-specific sculpture, or spirals of earth help us to imagine or organize livable worlds?

title: (S) Ruins & Reclamation
description: According to many environmental thinkers, we currently live among the ruins of an already-present environmental catastrophe. This not only means that processes currently set in motion will result in irreversible and irrevocable climatic harm to-come, but that depending from what perspective you look the contemporary appears in ruin. We will investigate how theorists and artists have understood the figure of the ruin as holding together the past with the present, so as to allow for a critical reading of current conditions. From a different angle, under the threat of rising seas and ecological disruption, literatures of environmental management take an interest in potentially ruined ecosystems in order to reclaim them. We will read restoration ecology, sustainability science, and ecological resilience to investigate the discourse of when ruined ecosystems are said to be renewed, recover, or otherwise able to resist further degradation. The class will promote site-based research and engagement off-campus, as we think together how practices of reclamation may help us read processes of ruination.

title: (S) Witchcraft: Madness, Visions, and Witnesses
description: Whether through speech, behavior, or social critique women have long shown opposition, both spiritual and political, to dominant forms of cultural life. In order to do so, the class turns to the figure of the “witch,” a figure who carries anxiety and fear about feminine forms of community and power. We look at that figure in popular culture, such as in the Wicked franchise, but also in historical texts that concern visions. At turns evil and unnatural, unruly and disorderly, but yet associated with the earth and with the feminine, we also consider how the “witch” has been an important figure in eco-feminism. – 3 – ACADEMIC STUDIES   [continued] The class attends to the production of fear and evil by looking at historical witch trials in New England; considers notions of insanity and female hysteria; as well as “mad” speech, such as that seen in witness or personal testimony. Finally, as we attempt to think outside of masculine logics of belief and reality, the class focuses on spells, curses and black magic.

title: (S) Sustainable Ecosystems: Art & Design Minor Capstone
description: The SEAD Capstone is designed to give students an opportunity to draw upon all of the Academic threads of the minor--art history, humanities, philosophy, and science--and develop a final research project that culminates in a studio project relevant to their SEAD studies. Each student will be expected to integrate environmental justice and ethics, sustainability, and design to produce a cohesive and practical arts-based project that addresses a local environmental issue. Through this project, students will demonstrate planning, communication, and leadership skills as well as a substantial studio effort. The projects may include a proposed design solution, an individual work or activist project made in response to a specific local climate concern or issue, etc. A statement of purpose and a project plan is required; the project concept and scope must be approved by the Coordinator of the SEAD Minor.

title: (PE) (S) The Biodesign Challenge: Concepts and Practice
description: This course employs art, design and scientific practice as sites of creative inquiry and civic responsibility. Students explore topics such as Speculative Design, Bioethics, Green Technology and the role of Art and Design in shaping Biofutures. The course emphasizes scientific literacy, critical analysis, ethical reasoning, and the translation of complex research into accessible, imaginative and impactful frameworks. This class centers on research, dialogue, and proposal development. Students formulate a focused research question, engage with scientific literature, consider feasibility and impact, and articulate a well-supported creative response grounded in systems thinking and responsible innovation. This course incorporates group work and presentations and will include one final, creative project. Students engage with scientific research as a foundation for creative development—no science background required. Note: This course offers a unique opportunity to develop a biodesign project for submission to the Biodesign Challenge (BDC), an international competition culminating in presentations at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York City. One project will be chosen to represent MECA&D at the annual BDC Summer Summit (all course grades are determined independently of this opportunity).

title: (PE) (S) Citizen Science
description: This project-based course explores how creative practitioners participate in and contribute to scientific inquiry at the local level. Grounded in citizen science and community-engaged research, students investigate pressing ecological issues within our surrounding coastal, forested, and/or agricultural environments. Drawing on MECA&D’s proximity to these areas and a network of life science experts, the course emphasizes collaboration, fieldwork, and direct ecological engagement. – 4 – ACADEMIC STUDIES   [continued] Students develop research-driven projects that respond to real conditions in our regional ecosystems, integrating observation, data collection, creative practice, and public communication. Through hands-on investigation the course positions art and design as active participants in environmental stewardship and community knowledge-building.

title: Degree Project
description: This course is devoted to independent, self-directed projects in graphic design, which are proposed, designed, written, and produced by each student on an individual basis. Faculty members serve as project advisors; this course is the studio component of the Senior Synthesis Seminar course; it also includes an “abridged synthesis writing” component for students choosing not to take a separate Senior Synthesis Seminar. Thesis projects involve visual research relating to a specific semester-long topic of visual communication. An appropriate form of presentation for the project in the Senior Thesis Exhibition and a separate written and visual summary culminate in book and/or website. Graphic Design Major requirement. 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Animation I
description: This course introduces and explores the twelve principles of animation as developed at the Disney Animation Studios. Assigned projects guide students through the technical and creative processes involved in 2D digital animation. Students apply the principles to create sequential drawings that give personality and emotion to moving objects and simple characters.

title: (PE) Public Engagement Minor Capstone
description: The Public Engagement Capstone with community-engaged component is a culminating experience for seniors in the minor. Students are encouraged to work independently, working with their chosen community partner prior to the capstone to develop a proposal. The faculty advisor, community partner and student meet prior, during and after the capstone project. The capstone is project based with a text component. Requirements of the text are determined with the faculty advisor in relationship to the work. Requirements for the capstone include a corollary statement of purpose, project plan, and studio component. Additionally, each capstone is shared publicly and  includes documentation that is permanently stored in the PE archive and MECA&D library. Students work with a faculty advisor under an independent study arrangement. Students can complete the Capstone requirement any time after their junior year, including summer.

title: (AE) Art & Entrepreneurship Minor Capstone
description: This course is a culminating experience for seniors in the Art & Entrepreneurship minor. Students will work with the minor coordinator to develop the concept and scope of the capstone project.

title: 3D Materiality: Woodworking 7-week class runs 1/11-2/26
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: 3D Materiality: Woodworking 7-week class runs 3/1-4/23
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Introduction to Drawing
description: This course introduces drawing from perception, including techniques for measurement, contour, massing, volume, and rendering illumination through value. Compositional skills such as placement and figure/ground interaction are stressed.  Drawing materials include vine charcoal, compressed charcoal, stamp, and india ink; subject matter includes still life, interior, landscape, and the figure.

title: Honors: Introduction to Drawing
description: The Honors section of Introduction to Drawing is a rigorous and demanding course designed for the advanced student. This course introduces drawing from perception, including techniques for measurement, contour, massing, volume, and rendering illumination through value. Compositional skills such as placement and figure/ground interaction are stressed. Drawing materials include vine charcoal, compressed charcoal, stamp, and India ink; subject matter includes still life, interior, landscape, and the figure.
